Last night, completed Bust 4 with a 1/2 tab of Vitamin L.  Have not had a legit attack since before Bust 3 but still experience soreness and occasional shadows, so I thought one more Bust would be appropriate.
On the Bust itself:
- significant body discomfort, experienced mostly as jaw and neck tension combined with agitation.  This was not a serious problem, but it was not especially pleasant either.  I paced quite a bit
- lots of visual changes in the form of shifting patterns and breathing floors, walls, etc
All in all,
though I have certainly had some good times, I am
ready for a break from The Vitamin L recreational experience.
medicinal effects:
- during the Bust, experienced occasional pressure in CH area.  This was not significant enough to qualify as a shadow.
That's about it!
The night before last, I went out to a nice dinner with my SO and another couple we've had planned for months.  We had a wine pairing with our dinner, so I had seven different wines, mostly reds, and a sweet wine with dessert.  I mention it because red wine is an almost guaranteed trigger for me as is anything overly sweet.  The meal passed without even a shadow!  I even had a couple martinis at cocktail hour before dinner.  I had an injection of Imitrex back in my room in case things broke bad, but did not have to think about it.
normally, I wouldn't dream of drinking so much as a beer during or around a CH cycle but with the success of the Vitamin L and D, I was able to enjoy myself.  It's a different world.  We've spent the past month wondering if we should cancel this dinner because of my cycle; quite empowering to go through with it like this.
*****
if anybody is reading this looking for advice:
-start the MM or L as soon as you can, and space them apart 5 days, per protocol.  I did 4 to avoid a Christmas Day Bust but I think 5 would have been better
-start the Vitamin D regime immediately. I eventually doubled my D to 20,000 ius a day but YMMV.  I'm a big guy.
-try aborting attacks with ice cold water and 5 hour energy drinks (these are far superior to any others, because you can bolt them down immediately).
-very strong ginger for shadows and neck massages at cluster spots, specifically, tendons on side of neck and under ear.  Strong coffee helped my shadows as well
-only take Imitrex if it's an emergency.  I have found I get significant increase in cluster attacks following Imitrex use and a marked increase in pain level
-keep a diary and be methodical.   When did your cycle start?  When does each attack start? How long does an attack last?  What did you do to stop it?  Use this to identify possible triggers and possible actions that help you.   It's also something to focus on and be constructive with when you're up in the middle of the night in excruciating pain
-find a doctor who will prescribe oxygen for you. It is not expensive out of pocket or through insurance.  Advocate for yourself on this or ask someone to help you advocate.

Hi everyone! I want to share my successful bust story and share what one should expect moving to or coming to Portugal with a CH.
A breif note on my case:
I have episodic CH
Last year, my doctor in Turkey prescribed me Cymbalta (Duloxetine, an SNRI) as an antidepressant. I am still taking 60 mg daily
I am a 6’6”, 200-pound male (which may or may not be relevant to the RC dose and side effects)
I moved to Portugal in early February, and my cycle started in mid-February
I hadn't had any busting experience before
CH treatment in Portugal:
You must have a prescription from a local doctor to get triptans. I assume a prescription from any doctor in Europe would be accepted, but mine was in Turkish and wasn’t accepted
In mid-February, the earliest available appointment with a neurologist in Porto or Braga was in mid-March. I went to a family doctor who gave me a prescription, but he could only prescribe a small number of pills, like high single digit. To get a sufficient supply for a full cycle, you need to see a neurologist
I was lucky enough to find an appointment in late February in a small town in northern Portugal. The doctor was quite knowledgeable and prescribed me an entire warchest of triptans. I think I now have enough for 2–3 bad cycles
Oxygen tanks are not easy to obtain here. You must be registered in the Portuguese healthcare system, and only a pulmonologist can prescribe them to you. The neurologist tried to get me prescription without me going to a pulmonologist, but ultimately failed to do so.
By the time I visited the neurologist, I had already ordered RC seeds. So, I decided to postpone jumping through all those hoops until later to prepare for the next cycle
My busting story:
I ordered RC seeds from
http://dutch-headshop.be
.
Thank you
@Shaun brearley
for sharing this website in another thread. I am sorry if it was originally posted by anyone else

Keep in mind that the website only accepts bank transfers, which means you need to allow 2–3 days for the payment to be processed. I received my seeds in 8 days—3 days for payment processing and 5 days for delivery.

I waited 5 days after taking my last triptan. I was lucky enough to have relatively mild attacks (as far as CH goes), so I managed without triptans and oxygen

I didn't have a trip or any side effects whatsoever.

Hellow to you all,
Since reading here on this forum about your successful stories and all the information that came with was very helpful to me, I want to return the favor and tell you mine.
I am 37 years old, live in germany and was first hit by the beast in my early twentys. After erratic outbursts in the beginning year, it settled at a 2 year cycle always starting in late autumn or winter, lasting 8 weeks with 3-4 attacks per 24h. (reading a while an this board, I now consider myself pretty lucky)
Every cycle i would go to a different doktor or neurologist and was sent home with various migraine pills. When I mentioned CH as a possibility, I was shut down by the dokters, because that couldnt be because did not show any facial paralysis… wich I actually dont, even at the worst „banging-my-head-against-something-hard-and-cold-pain“. So I went on popping chewed up Sumatriptan-Pills, untill the dokters wouldnt perscribe any more.
In 2022 I finally found a neurologist who`s first guess was CH. In his words: „What else should it be!?“ At that appointment my last cycle was over for about 2 months, so we agreed to get the O2 at the next cycle and maybe try cortisone infusions… the first idiea i liked, the second one not so much. So I startet searching the internet and found this forum. Thank you for all the hope you gave me.
Due to experiences in my younger years, I decided to try MM at the next cycle. And here is how it went so far.
The cycle startet at 28th December 2023. My cycle started very slowly with only 2 hits every 2 days in the first week.
First bust 06th January:
1,5g powderd MM in chocolate… turns out it works like lemon tekk, so it was a bit more intense then I planned. While coming down I got immediatly hit with a really bad slapback lastig 3h and 2h later again same as hard for 2h. The following night was quiet, but untill the next bust i had every night 2-3 attacks. None at daytime, only mild shadows.
Second bust 12th January:
1 g in chocolate. Had bad hadaches (not CH, but from all the sleep deprevation) while busting, felt terrible and the slapbacks came on like the fist time. Again i got hit the following days every night 2-3 times. At that point my spirits startet to leave me and I was questioning the whole process. Again reading all the similar experiences here kept me going.
Third bust 17th January:
1,25g same chocolate… That day was from the beginning out oft he ordinary. Had an strong but clear and short hit in the night and then shortly after getting up. That one was less painfull but would not go away untill noon. Every time i thought it would fade, it ramped up again. In the evening just before I took the MM i felt it coming on again, but it was stopped by the MM.
Coming down I felt the slapback slowly starting to burn in my face, but then fading away… that happend twice or more in the next hour and suddenly I had the strong feeling, that there wont be any more attacks. Like my body knows now how to keep it from getting out

What sets the HP Gland to go off its regular schedule from 1x a day?
Barometric Pressure from one of the following  causes but not limited to:
Change in season - lengthening of days Spring to Summer, Summer to Fall, Fall to Winter
Geographical location - by the coast you get lots of low/high pressure changes from storms or marine layer or fog
Weather changes - low pressure from storms (high pressure is usually good as it is stable)
Stress - adds up to pressure in the head which affects the HP
irregular sleeping patterns
stimulants
Why is it important to keep it on a regular schedule?
When it goes off from the body's clock, trouble starts for CH people.
For example let's say I have four CH per day like clockwork at
11am
4pm
11pm
4am
this is HP (Hypothalamus Gland) triggering at the wrong time
Here's what I think is going on...
At 11am the HP is triggered for the second time today. It is off the from the body's clock. They should be in sync going off at the same time. This would be my normal waking time.  We all have one natural rising time, but two different alarms in the form of an HP clock and Body clock. Hopefully they go off once a day, together in sync.
My HP clock is operating too fast
I think that as opposed to a trigger, the headache has come about because the HP clock has been "Speed Up".  The change in seasons confused it and now it is running to fast. HP clock thinks a day is only six hours long instead of 24. It is moving too fast.
Now the HP gland is triggering at 11 rather than at my natural time is because it is off.  In my case it is already on the second alarm in a single day! My body is getting the message to wake up, its the start of the day again. The rest of the body realizes it is already almost mid day. The body clock is working fine. It reacts to the message and says "terrorist" and sends its defenses to attack. Instantly the histamines flow and everything gets worse now. Chemical imbalances in the body. Pressure builds in your sinuses. This sinus pressure puts pressure on and triggers the TG nerve. Now the pain receptors have been flooded by a "key " molecule that fits into them. This molecule we will call K. It is the molecule that fits in the receptor causing a protein P to be released in the brain saying "OW! HOLY HELL THIS HURTS!"
Your body clock does not quit fighting. It continues to send messages and fight the imaginary terrorist trying to wake up the body for no reason.
Your brain is flooded by K everywhere. all your pain receptors are being blown up and sucked out of the protein P.
You feel the headache as long as K is filled and triggering P to your brain.
The good news is P runs out. That is when your headache stops. Some people hold a lot of P some hold a little. This is called tolerance of Pain. you have a  lot of P you will suffer a lot of pain. You have a little P, you will have pain but shorter headaches. I have a lot of P. My headaches can be minimum 2-4 hours and then 2-4 more sitting around as the P leav

First post but have been reading the board for years. I wanted to share what has been working for me for the past year and a half. Usual caveats- not a doctor, your mileage may vary, and this is just my experience.
For some background, I have had cluster headaches for going on 12 years. Every year has a minimum of two episodes that last 8-10 weeks. Each episode will have typically 2-3 headaches per day. Emgality and verapamil didn’t prevent or stop clusters for me. I have had strong side effects from medicines like sumatriptan and Topamax and refused to use them any longer.
I tried the clusterbuster program and had mild success on my on days, but immediately fell back into headaches on the days off with no lasting relief.
Last year I tried the below supplement routine and have used it for my last three cycles. I have had shadows on the program but have been headache free for 18 months so far. It’s based on research showing low allopregnanolone levels in cluster headache patients and theorizing that the low levels are a part of the headache process
Treatment Plan:
I start at first sign of headaches or shadows.
Reducing the Severity of Headaches and Shadows:
aka preventing the trigeminal nerve from getting angry
Cetirizine:
10mg twice daily (h1 receptor antagonist. Low potential to cross blood brain barrier however some research shows it will slightly. Chosen over benedryl due to lack of sedation and cognitive impairment)
Famotidine:
20mg twice daily (h2 receptor antagonist, crosses blood brain barrier)
Quercetin:
500mg twice daily. (reduced global histamine response)
Raising Allopregnanolone levels and improving HTPA function:
to help fill in the gaps where the body is currently failing in production
Vitamin D3 with K2:
10,000mg of Vitamin D3 with 300mg of K2, taken once daily.
Pregnenolone:
50mg once daily to convert to allopregnanolone. Research shows pregnenolone readily converts to allopregnanolone. This can be titrated up as needed to reduce headache frequency and severity. I have gone up to 300mg during a cycle, and there is some research showing that it is well tolerated up to 500mg per day.
DHEA:
50mg daily to encourage the conversion of pregnenolone to allopregnanolone so it doesn’t convert down the testosterone pathway.
Ashwagandha:
250mg sensoril for reduced cortisol
Abortive:
At the first sign of a headache
Caffeine:
from coffee or tea. I avoid caffeine sources such as energy drinks or supplements containing niacin or other vasodilators, which is counter productive since we want the vasoconstriction of caffeine
Every 5 days step down pregnenolone dosage. If shadows return, step back up and repeat.
With this I have been able to keep my headaches away and combat shadows with a cup of coffee.
This is my third cycle using this program and I waited to post it until I had repeat success. I understand that this could be unique to me, however the repeated success I’ve had has made me want to share this protocol in case it can help anyone else.

Hey Clusterbusters
My name is Alec and I'm 33 years old. I had my first cluster headache at 12 and was diagnosed at 16 by a neurologist where I live in Australia. I have been a member of several forums a few years back but haven't really been active in support groups for the last 5 years or so and I need to vent. I saw the "Beast" documentary on YouTube a few days ago and Cluster Busters seems like an awesome group - I'd love to become an active member here and get involved as much as I can. I have just had a KIP 10 cluster attack with a complete absence of pain (see my story below), I could just feel the immense pressure and I can tell what pain level it is even though it's masked. I haven't had a KIP10 in around 6 months or so and it literally just went away, so I'm still pumping full of adrenalin and just want to talk a bit about my last few months.
When I was diagnosed at 16, I attempted the full array of then first and second line treatment options. All the standard medications such as verapamil, triptans etc. My headaches never responded to any preventatives or abortives and as a last resort I was placed on opioids. At first I was taking codeine and then graduated to oxycodone. I want to make it clear from the outset that I do not recommend opioids for cluster headaches sufferers, but every situation is unique and for me opioids became my primary way of coping with my headaches. Of course, the dose continually increased and to be completely honest up until around a month ago they never really provided me with much relief from the physical pain at all. They did help me markedly with the emotional distress and turmoil associated with treatment-refractory cluster headaches which unfortunately for me became chronic at around 25 years old.
I understand that everybody's perception of pain is different and that some of us have episodic clusters and others (like me) have chronic. I have to say though that after doing research on cluster headaches online, either there are many people out there who claim to have the condition who clearly do not OR I happen to suffer a lot more than others. For me, cluster headaches are the worst imaginable pain and trying to describe them is pointless. I do not understand how somebody could have cluster headaches and mistakenly think they have migraines for years before a correct diagnosis - for me, the pain of a cluster headache is so distinct and unique.
The toll these headaches have had on my life is catastrophic. I have been in 2x long-term relationships with people I loved and both ended due to my inability to be a normal person due to my pain. I have tried so hard to maintain employment and have worked in several industries including law enforcement, finance and legal services. All of my attempts at employment have ended due to my pain and I have been on long-term disability now for several years. 21 year Episodic. Clusters used to happen every 9 months, lasted 1 month with 1 or two attack / day lasting 1 hour each. The cycle would be like clockwork, almost to the day. Back then I was treating with maxalt wafers only, and running cold water on my head. Eventually was prescribed amitriptyline, which unsurprisingly didn't do anything. Sometimes my cycle would
end
with a day-long attack. Different from a regular cluster headache, but, still on the cluster side. Really weird. Anyway, I would often end up in the ER with these ending attacks, but, they were always bad news / good news. Bad because I knew they would last at least 24 hours; and were brutal (not as bad as a CH attack, but, pretty unbearable due to the length of the attack and no sleep). Good because I knew the cycle would end after that one weird one. Eventually saw my first neurologist in 2012 after a trip to the ER (10 years after my CHs started). I've had all of the required brain scans, etc. and got the official diagnosis. Neurologist recommended maxalt wafers, home oxygen, prednisone taper at the start of a cycle, and verapamil for the duration of the cycle and for 6 weeks after it clears. Family doctor wouldn't prescribe me any of those things except for Maxalt. Said everything else was too risky.
Found a new Family Doctor 2 years later (2014). Got me hooked up with another Neurologist, and got the same treatment plan recommended. Got all of my scripts filled, etc.
Over the years, my cycles spread out longer (longer remission), but, more attacks while in cycle. One cycle, I didn't realize the risks of too many triptans (was taking 2, 3, sometimes 4 per day that cycle). Ended up getting really bad rebound headaches. Cycle lasted much longer, and I ended up getting upwards of 8 attacks per day. After that cycle, and realizing what had happened; I've tried to avoid the triptans whenever possible. I rely on home o2, and only use a maxalt nasal spray if I'm out and don't have access to o2 or any other options.
Started the D3 regimen in 2019, and have been taking all of the cofactors and everything daily since then.
For the last few cycles, my treatment plan has been:
1) 13 day prednisone taper at the onset of a cycle; 60mg 2 days, 50mg 3 days, 40mg 3 days, 30mg 2 days, 20mg 2 days, 10mg 1 day
2) Verapamil - 120mg x 3 / day
3) Home o2 - 25lpm through Cluster o2 mask (coupled with the odd sip of really cold ice water through a straw)
4) Rizatriptan Nasal spray only for emergencies (5mg)
5) Still doing the D3 regimen
6) Have tried adding melatonin in before bed (10mg)
7) Energy drinks to try to help clear shadows
8) Ice-pack on the head for shadows, and sometimes during attacks (other times I can't stand it)
9) No alcohol
The prednisone taper has killed 2 cycles right at the start. One cycle it didn't completely kill it (the cycle above with the triptan overuse); and I ended up doing a 2nd prednisone taper, which did end it

Hey,
I’m 21 year old male, 6’4/230lbs. I have been having episodes of cluster headaches lasting around 2 months for the past couple of years.
First time it happened (around 4 years ago), I went to several doctors and I was misdiagnosed with having a high blood pressure and I was prescribed daily medication to decrease it. I knew that the doctor planned to keep me on it for a very long time but I took it for a few months and I just stopped it. I did not plan to take it for my rest of my life. Surprisingly, at that time those medications decreased the intensity of my headaches and eventually it stopped.
The next year, I moved to study abroad in Montreal, Canada. At around December/January, I started having headaches all over again. I usually do dental work on my teeth and I thought that it might be caused from one of my tooth. After weeks fighting the headache and going to dentists, I resolved to the conclusion that I had to remove my wisdom tooth and eventually the headaches stopped. (I was prescribed lots of painkillers I remember a Codeine/paracetamol that I took daily along with sleep aids)
The year after, I was in my home country and I started getting the same problem all over again, I checked on my vision, I went to every doctor possible and at the end, I was referred to a neurologist after at least 3-4 weeks of daily headaches. That was the day I understood what is CH. I was prescribed with the regular routine including Prednisone. And the intensity of the headache kept decreasing till it eventually stopped.
I spent the following cycle with the same routine, however I did not want to take prednisone. Both my parent’s families had diabetes, including all the grandparents.
—-
3-4 weeks ago, my cycle started but I spent the first 10 days with very mild headaches that I could easily ignore and they usually go in 30-40 mins. I thought that I could just keep waiting and my whole cycle will stay at this intensity and indeed I did not take any single medication and kept waiting.
Right after the first 10 days, I had a really extreme headache, my body was extremely sweating, I was in a terrible amount of pain and It lasted at least 3 hours. Once that happened, I went online and ordered 5g of dried Vitamin M in capsules of 0.5g. Since that time my headaches intensity changed from almost no pain to 7-10/10 pain. Every day I would wake up, I will get a headache in exactly 1-2 hours and I would have to sleep and take painkillers and waste at least 3-4 to recover and start studying for my midterms. I insisted on not taking any triptans or prednisone although I has them right them right in front of me.
4 days later, (daily 1x headache 7-9 pain) I took my first Vitamin M dose of 1g right after I woke up (empty stomach). it skipped my morning headache but I was hit with another headache later at night. (I assumed it was a slap back)
I hoped my cycle would just stop or at least decrease in intensity but nothing happened. I started continuing my daily headaches

Harmala Alkaloids are substances found in varying degrees in plants like Syrian Rue, Banisteriopsis caapi vine, Passionflower and Tobacco (I'm sure there are more)Â  The two with the highest known amounts are the Syrian Rue seeds and the Banisteriopsis Caapi vines.
http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Harmala_alkaloid
The major alkaloids involved are Harmine, Harmaline, and Tetrahydroharmine.Â  These are all Beta Carbolines, They all inhibit the MAO A enzyme, and they all are considered RIMA's--Reversible Inhibitors of MAO subtype A.Â (For more info on RIMA's you can look into the pharmaceutical anti-depressant moclobomide, which has MAO A enzyme as it's mechanism of action.)Â  Â All 3 substances are completely legal in the U.S., as are both the Caapi Vine and Rue seeds.Â  I know I have read that they are hitting some of the same receptors as Psilocybin and LSA, but right now I can't find my sources.
But first we'll touch on MAO inhibitors in general.Â  Everything you hear about them sounds scary, everything seems to say, "Do Not Mix With MAO inhibitors"Â  We need to know that the substances contained in Caapi vines and Syrian Rue seeds are very different drugs than the old style pharmaceutical MAO inhibitors that were usually used for depression.Â  These older MAO inhibitors would inhibit TWO enzymes, MAO A and MAO B, where as the RIMA's only inhibit ONE enzyme, MAO A.Â  This is important because that means you still have an active MAO B enzyme, which makes your body perfectly able to still metabolize Tyramine.Â  With the older MAO inhibitors that inhibited BOTH enzymes you are not able to metabolize tyramine, this can lead to hypertensive crisis, and this is the biggest warning you will hear about with MAO inhibiting drugs.Â  Just remember--THIS IS NOT AN ISSUE WITH THE NATURAL MAO A INHIBITING DRUGS LIKE RUE OR CAAPI VINES.Â  The biggest thing that made me more comfortable with these drugs was when I realized that I have been taking them all my life without knowing it.Â  Certain foods, like chocolate, have this effect naturally andÂ  you'll never notice it.Â  Â Â In fact, Terence Mckenna claims that ALL tryptamines are RIMA's--this means the mushrooms, LSD, and LSA containing seeds that we are taking are all having this effect on us.Â  I can confirm this effect from the experience of smoking DMT while on mushrooms.Â  (More on that later)
So no risk of "hypertensive crisis"Â  Just forget about it.Â  There is the theory that if you are on RIMA's and eat lots and lots of tyramine containing foods that eventually you could "overwhelm" the system and cause this crisis, but I have NEVER heard of this actually happening.Â  From what I've read it seems more like people have been scared of MAO inhibitors for a long time and are reluctant to let go of that fear.Â  One of the biggest sources of tyramine is chees

I am new here and wanted to share my experience with CH.  This site has truly changed my life and I want to return the favor for anyone that comes along.
I am 40 and have had migraines all of my life.  It runs in my family.  Large doses of caffeine and/or sumatriptan would get rid of these quickly and it didn't happen often.  No biggie - 5 time a year on average.
However, 5 years ago, I started experiencing a type of pain I didn't know even existed.  I have been to the E.R., had many MRI's, visits with headache specialists, neurologists, chiropractors, and nut jobs.  At no point did anyone know what was going on with me.  I was told it was just a migraine.  No sir!! I know what a migraine is.  Those are bad but this...this is worse than anything I can imagine.  This type of pain made me cry in front of my kids...sigh..wish that hadn't happened!  Made me want to physically hurt anyone who spoke to me.  It wasn't until this year that I found this site and wow!  Things have changed.
Year 1
- Started late summer.
- Pain levels of 8-10 ever night at 7pm almost to the dot.
- Could sleep.
- Terrible "shadow" for the entire day.  Repeat cycle.
- Went to doctor after doctor.  Sumatriptan helped reduce most pain 2 or 3 points down.
- During one attack I noticed how stuffy my nose was and that I couldn't breathe.  I was desperate.  Found an old bottle of Afrin and almost emptied the bottle in my nose.  Attack gone in 15 minutes.
- 7 months into it I went to an ENT.  Said I needed surgery.  I did.  Doctor aggressively trimmed down my turbinates. 2 weeks later - all attacks gone.
Year 2
- nada.  Nothing.  Life was awesome.
Year 3
- Year 1 repeated itself but on the other side.  Went back to ENT, had another surgery.  Same results.
Year 4
- nada.  Nothing.  Life was again normal.  Had some attacks that seemed random but mostly triggered by alcohol.  It took me a while to realize that was a trigger.  Saw neuro who said I had CH but he wasn't sure that was even a real thing.  Gave me Sumatriptan.  Whatever.  Attacks were on and off throughout the year and it was bearable.
Year 5 (current)
- started having small attacks after drinking with friends.  I immediately knew what was going down.
- by Mid-August I was having 5 attacks/day. (post wakeup, 3ish, 7ish, 2am waking from sleep.  Pain levels of 6,7,10,8 respectively.  Each one lasting 2-3 hours.  I am not sure life could be worse at the moment.
- by end of August I was taking 5 times the recommended dose of Sumatriptan.  It was enough to keep me sane but I used 30 days worth in 1 week.  Doc refused to refill.
- Desperate, I started searching and found this site.  I can't tell you how many times I read through these forums while pacing through my house.  Called my PCP and demanded an appointment immediately.  They told me it would be 4-5 weeks.  I called again after 2 days and begged.  They saw me the next day.  I asked for o2.  Doc was confused but wrote a script anyway.
Today, I am almost completely pain free f

Hey folks!
First let me say that I'm grateful to find these forums, and the resources in the relevant stickied topics/links already taught me plenty I didn't know about this horrible thing, and some hope to be had in a moment I really, really need.
I thought I'd share my history with CH, since even those who empathize and care for me don't really understand what it's like. I was one of those people that almost never got headaches naturally, and even when I was hungover it was not that common, unless I had a LOT to drink, which predictably became less and less common as I aged. I'm 29 years old and I live in Brazil, and in 2017 I got my first episode of CH, and of course I had no idea what that was, it lasted a week, and came at 3h30 in the morning and went away exactly an hour later, like the world's most brutalizing clockwork. After three days I was absolutely desperate, as anything I'd take had no effect whatsoever, before or during the attack, but I couldn't get an appointment at a neurologist covered by my insurance plan until a week later, so I endured the agony. Fortunately, he did know what CH was, which after a cursory reading of the experiences on this forum seems rather uncommon, but unfortunately he didn't know how to treat it properly. He prescribed Naratriptan each day before bed, and intranasal oxygen at a hospital, but I didn't have time to practice any of it, as it had gone away and I hoped it would never return.
7 months later, in February of 2018, came the second episode, exactly like the first one. The Naratriptan did exactly nothing, and as I lived alone, not in a million years would I be able to get to a hospital by myself, much less in time to be treated for something that lasted an hour. Fuck, I couldn't even get to the bathroom. But despite that, I had one hope: That it would only be six days more of agony, and then hopefully it would leave me alone, and it did. After that I went to another neurologist, and he recommended Sumatriptans, but otherwise mantained the same useless oxygen prescription. A year later in 2019 it came back again, same ol' story, and for the first two days I tried the Sumatriptan pills, which again did nothing, but as I was recently diagnosed with T2 Diabetes, I had a lot of syringes at home, so I bought Sumatriptan vials and started to apply it myself, starting with small doses and adjusted, until I came to the conclusion that 4mg was enough. I'd apply it and smoke enough weed to down an elephant, and I'd get a fast relief and fall asleep soon after, although I'd feel like shit the next day, but hey, it was just for a week and it was lightyears better than going through that agony over and over again, plus I thought that banging my head against the wall couldn't be good for my brain.

The time has come to introduce myself to our beloved clusterheads family.
I was observing You for long time, but now I feel obliged to give my input.
My name is Radoslaw and I'm from Poland, suffering for CH since 2016 (maybe 2015, I don't remember properly). First two years without proper diagnose, but with small 1-2 weeks episodes during autumn each year. In 2018 I had 4 weeks terrible headaches, so I started my journey through neurologists. With help form the web knowledge I made a diagnose which was later confirmed by doctors - cluster headaches. Firstly doctors proposed me verapamil, but after cardiac research they refused to give it to me because of bradicardia ( a lot of wrestling and boxing in the teenage years made my hearth rate about 50-55). I didn't want to take lithium and gabapentin because of side effects ( no other therapy was known here in PL)
In 2019 in June my cluster appeared once again, stronger than before, so I desperately tried to find new solution. I found new doctor ( who isn't neurologist but is a specialist in headaches - brilliant guy !). My hearth rate felt down to 45-50 ( during night even to 40) but I felt good, without side effects, so I decided to continue it. For next 2 years I even forgot that I have this terrible disease ( for my bad...). Unfortunately I didn't stop taking verap after cluster period, but stayed on it for the whole year. In 2021 each attempt to reduce verap caused some shadows and single attacks, so my doctor suspected that I went to chronic version ( today I'm wondering that maybe I'm not chronic, but such long period of verap caused attacks when I tried to stop it - slowly as recommended).

First CH's started November of 2015.  Thought they were a result of a hangover, caffeine withdrawals, or dehydration.  True cycles started to emerge in spring 2016.  Misdiagnosed by ER as Migraine.  Correctly diagnosed as CH later.  Went through the cycle of preventative and abortives.  Benefits were often limited, side effects were intolerable.  Methodically tried elimination diets, med shifts, etc.  Most with some degree of correlation, but none with lasting relief.  Latest preventative was visiting a local chiropractor.  He noted in Chiropractic school, a member of his cohort was a CH suffer and they focused a bit of Chiropractic intervention with success. I gave it a try.  (Will add notes at the bottom on the impact of other meds)
When engaging chiropractor, I chose to not disclose much beyond CH.  He ran an x-ray panel and review with me.  In his review, he commented that the misalignment of C1 to C3, and other issues in the cervical vertebrae and other markers would lead him to believe I had suffered whiplash ~10 years earlier.  I had not disclosed in Dec of 2002, I had a head-on collision that I chose not to pursue medical treatment for.  He proceeded with a 3-month cycle of adjustments, 3x weekly, with a focus on addressing cervical issues.
In the 3 month cycle, which started about 2 weeks after the start of my CH cycle, the headaches stopped almost entirely.  3 months in, I have a few breaks through CHs,  average 1 or fewer per day (which is miraculous as on cycle I am consistently at 3x daily, on a schedule [2:31 am, 6:00 am, and 2:00 pm]).  I've noted that, when I miss the Chiro adjustments (due to work, etc) greater than 4 days, there is a higher potential to suffer more than 1 CH.
As we all know, CH triggers and resolution is mostly about correlation, as causality is not yet well established.  But thus far, chiropractic adjustments, coupled with stretches, shoulder and neck muscle exercises focusing on extension / relaxation, with heat on neck seem to have demonstrated the most effective treatment path for prevention without side effects.

I’m 56 years old, and I suffer from episodic cluster headaches. It took years to get properly diagnosed, and by the time I did, the condition had already taken over large parts of my life. Anyone who has experienced cluster headaches understands that this isn’t just a headache—it’s a cycle of severe, debilitating pain that dictates how you live day to day.
Before I found an effective system, I relied on multiple treatments just to function. I used Rizatriptan, which could take up to an hour to work. Then I moved to Sumatriptan nasal spray, which worked in about 25 minutes, and eventually injectable Sumatriptan, which was faster. I also used high-flow oxygen at home set to 15 liters per minute, and I carried a portable unit at work. Even with all of this, my life revolved around anticipating attacks—how many injections I had left, where I could go, and whether I could even leave the house.
Preventive therapy helped stabilize things. I currently take Verapamil daily and use Emgality monthly. These treatments made a difference, but they didn’t completely free me from the cycles.
After doing a lot of research, I came across information from groups like Clusterbusters and studies being done at Yale. I read about people using psilocybin in different ways—microdosing, moderate dosing, and high-dose “cycle-breaking” approaches. After careful consideration, I chose the high-dose method.
My first experience was a full weekend: 7 grams on Friday, 7 grams on Saturday, and 7 grams on Sunday. What I noticed immediately, the intensity was way less and they started to come back slowly, not immediate. Another surprising change was how my oxygen therapy worked. Before, it would take 15 to 20 minutes to stop an attack. After that weekend, when I have any symptoms at all, oxygen worked in about 5 minutes. That alone was life-changing.
Over the course of a year, I repeated this same three-day protocol two more times, spacing each weekend several months apart. Each time, the result was consistent: the cluster headaches stopped. When they eventually returned, they came back slower, less intense, and much more manageable.
At one point, I stopped my preventive medications to see what would happen. The cluster headaches came back. When I restarted my medications, they went away again. That confirmed for me that my baseline treatment—Verapamil and Emgality—is still essential.
What I’ve learned from this journey is that it’s not one single solution—it’s a combination. Preventive therapy keeps things stable. Acute treatments like oxygen and triptans provide backup when needed. And for me, psilocybin appeared to interrupt the cycle in a way nothing else had.
Today, my life is very different. I’m no longer trapped by my condition the way I once was. I still respect it, I still prepare for it, but I have tools that work—and that has given me a level of control I didn’t think was possible before.
This is my personal experience. It’s not a recommendation or a guideline, but it is

Hi all,
New to the forum, but geesh am I happy to have found it. A little background about my experience with cluster headaches:
My first cluster headache was when I was kid, like 13 or so. I woke up in the middle of the night and thought I was dying. The pattern or cycles were pretty irregular until my mid-twenties (I am 32 now), and now I seem to go through a cycle about every year or so, though I haven't been fastidious enough to notice when they happen, such as in the summer, spring, fall, etc. The cycles themselves seem to last for 3 weeks to a month.
The headaches themselves typically happen in the middle of the night, and then I have a shadow (a term I learned thanks to this forum!) off and on during the day. The usually are on the right side of my head, I don't think they've ever happened on my left, and last for about an hour to an hour and a half. Lastly, and I'm not really proud of this, I never went to the doctor about it until this year when I get my diagnosis (I know, I know, I should've gone sooner).
So that's my background, now onto my question. My most recent cycle started about three weeks ago. It started like it typically does -- attacks would happen at night (sometimes midnight, sometimes 2 am, sometimes 10 pm -- you never know) they last for over an hour, at worst 2 hours, and then would go away and I would sleep the rest of the night. This cycle seems to be worse in terms of frequency of attacks, which is why I ended up going to the doctor. My doctor prescribed me with sumatriptan, which has been effective-ish.
Historically I would get attacks once at night, maybe once in the morning right when I am about to wake up, and then have a shadow for most of the day. This time around, I am getting them at night almost every 2 - 4 hours. This last week in particular has been a special kind of hell. Last night I got one of my worst ones around 8 pm, it lasted until about 9, then I tried to go to sleep. I woke up with the beginnings of another attack around 10 - 10:30 pm, took sumatriptan and it seemed to recede. Then at 5 am I got another one, not as bad but still not fun. My doctor prescribed 10 tablets of sumatriptan last Friday, and I went through all of them in a weekend (yikes, I know). My doctor recently prescribed me Topamax, which I've not started because from reading about all the side effects, it sounds like a nightmare. As well as the increase the past 2 weeks or so, I've been getting low grade headaches/achiness all day with intense pressure. We've also been dealing with the run-off of Hurricane Ian, which I am assuming is not helping.
My question is, has anyone else dealt with the increase of frequency or the crappy achiness/pressure? Is this a sign that I am entering the last phases of the cycle? How often do you take sumatriptan during cycles? Any experience with Topamax?
I'm getting an MRI next weekend, so we'll see what the tells me.

During my research, I discovered cluster headaches. I always said I got migraines in clusters, not knowing that there was a difference. I can't tell you the overwhelming emotional feeling that I have finally found a community of people who match what I live through. It sounds crazy to say, but I'm actually excited. I am in a cluster season now, and just the possibility of new treatments brings chills and tears and fear of still not working. But I just want to say hello, and thank you for more knowledge about my condition in the last 3 weeks, than I've had in 26 years. For now, I try to medicate as little as possible, since the only thing that ever really touched my headaches was imitrex but with the frequency of headaches, I would take more than I was supposed to and starting getting extremely long rebound headaches, the last one reaching 8 hours. That was the last time I ever let myself take it. Beyond the rebounds, I also noticed my "migraine seasons" as I called them, would last 3 months instead of my normal 2. Backtracking, sorry 26 years is a long time to be in the dark, when my headaches started, I would get 1-3 and sometimes even 5 a day. Pretty consistently with only a handful of completely pain free days. That lasted till I was about 16. I honestly am racking my brain to try and remember the first time I took a hit of lsd. I think it really might have been around the time my headaches started coming every 6 months. Anyway, when I left the neurologist, I was still suffering every six months. After getting off all the medications, my headaches started coming once a year to year and a half. Either spring or fall. That is what I have been living with for the last 18 years. Once a year, for 2 solid months, I get what I now know to be cluster headaches. The first time I feel the heat in my temple start, I know, the next two months, are going to be hell. I try to drink water and suffer through like a freaking crazy person. Sometimes I take muscle relaxers because every muscle in the right side of my head is knotted into a ball. I'm not sure how to start this but here goes nothing. I am a 27 year old female who has been having episodic clusters since my early twenties. They started out short and intense, but would resolve quickly. Then they got longer and longer and more intense and I was having upwards of eight attacks a day, usually every summer. My family and I assumed they were migraines and I would just lock myself away in the coolest places I could find and try to ride them out. Excedrin never worked and I would usually throw it up anyway. I can't keep food down, so when I am in a cluster cycle I end up losing a lot of weight. If I would get an attack at work I would run to the bathroom, shut off the lights and press my head into the nearest cool surface. It was devastating for me when family, friends, and coworkers asked me why I couldn't just work through the pain. I felt weak and pathetic. It wasn't until nursing school that I learned that these were clusters and I felt hope that there was treatment out there for me. When I discovered oxygen therapy I just about cried with relief because I wasn't sure how much longer I could handle it. Since using oxygen I had no idea how I survived going so long without it but eventually, even the oxygen wasn't enough. Sometimes it would work, other times it wouldn't. I tried altering my breathing perfectly only for my cluster to rebound stronger and it crushed me. I was losing the energy to even try to find new ways to breath through my non-rebreather mask for relief. Then came my Imitrex injections which were heaven sent. My NP was unfamiliar with the condition and prescribed me multiple doses. I knew that they don't recommend taking more than for doses in a month period, but I didn't care so I didn't inform her. To me, the relief was worth any risk. Eventually she learned this and called me to inform me that I wasn't supposed to take so many doses, but how could I not. The pain is so extreme that there aren't enough words to describe it and the Imitrex can take it away in seconds. Luckily my remission periods are getting longer, but my clusters are getting less consistent. I now can get them at any season and at any time and they don't seem to fit any pattern. When I am in the middle of a cluster I am constantly praying that God either take the pain away or finally let me die, even though outside of the cluster I still want to live. I'm currently taking a break from nursing and I no idea how the hell I'm supposed maintain that career with such an unreliable condition. I don't have a spouse or children, but my family is aware of my clusters. They are supportive, but when I miss out on events I can tell they are frustrated with me, It's not their fault, but I hate being in public mushing my face against dirty toilets while strangers try to ask if I'm ok. I'm not. I'm tired. I want to be done.

I felt compelled to share my story after seeing Bob Wold present at Horizons 2009 tonight on vimeo. This is my first post. I welcome your comments. Please forgive me if I have inadvertently violated any forum rules or guidelines, or if I am repeating common knowledge.
NB: I am not a doctor. I am an opinionated total stranger writing under a pseudonym. Nothing I am saying could or should be construed as medical advice.
I started getting severe headaches when I was very young. One day, when I was about five, my parents gave me the usual 2 acetaminophen and sent me to bed. About 20 minutes later, I came out of my room in tears. Exasperated, they wanted to know why. I told them because I couldn't handle the pain, and that if felt like someone was stabbing me in the eyes. Off to the doctor we went.
There was never really an answer to the headache question. At first, I was prescribed Fioricet (acetaminophen+butalbital+caffeine).  The headaches were called tension, then migraine, then cluster, then migraine. I was more interested in ending the pain then having an accurate label.
I do also get normal headaches, which are a totally different beast. No eye pain, no light sensitivity. I pop 2 aspirin+acetaminophen+caffeine and go about my day. If they get bad but not really bad, my favorite self-remedy is standing in a warm shower in a dark bathroom with my face directly under the spray until it stops. I also do this during really bad ones.
Some of the worse headaches were the ones that woke me up. I would usually be somewhat confused; and I would often spend a while in bed in terrible pain before I would get up and take pills. Sometimes, the pain would be entangled in a dream, and I would think if I could solve a math problem, or dial a phone, or repot a plant (I can remember those three dreams to this day, and they happened between 10 and 25 years ago), the pain would stop.
Later in life, they switched me to Zomig (zolmitriptan). The Zomig works ~75% of the time. When it doesn't work, it makes the pain worse. Currently, my insurance won't pay for Zomig, and so I have sumatriptan (Imitrex), which I haven't had to try yet, as I have one Zomig left.
When I was about 19, I entered another stage. The headaches became more common and lasted longer. The pain was worse.
I distinctly remember the worse one I ever had (and I bet you do, too.) It was 1999, and I was 21. The pain started when I was at work, about 6 hours from the end of my shift. It was impossible not to cry when I started reading many of your messages.Â  Crying is something I pay dearly for, I can already feel the tips of my fingers turning into icicles and I know what's coming next.Â
Here's my story...
I'm am pretty sure I was born with a migraine.Â  My mother said I cried for 6 months straight.Â  (maybe it was my destiny, maybe it was because I was a high forcep delivery, I did have bruises on my temples for 6 weeks my mom said) She said one day I just stopped crying and started talking! At 7 months old.Â  I was reading and writing by 3.Â  Then, my first memories of headaches started at about 4.Â  I remember burying my head in my couch and in the corner of the back seat of my car.Â  I threw up all the time.Â  And it took 2 days to recover each time.Â  I missed school weekly from kindergarten to 3rd grade.Â  At the age of 6 they hospitalized me for a week to do all sorts of testing.Â  The conclusion was migraines. They had me on phenobarbital for a long time.Â  I think close to a year.Â  Then at 7 they very very slowly weened me off. I was headache free from ages 8 to 11.Â
Two things happened at 11.Â  We moved from Vermont to Tennessee.Â  And, I started getting my pre-menstrual hormones.Â  I have two type A parents and I was constantly called lazy in the summer and once a month when I got my period.Â  I just couldn't function.Â  But I tried to just push through.Â  I started popping exedrin like candy as soon as I could buy them myself at 14.Â  Then we moved to Colorado, and the heat was never that bad.Â  And it always cooled down at night.Â  So, I just got used to having really bad migraines one week a month, on hot days, whenever I cried and when big storm pressures came in.Â  I just took exedrin and imitrex and managed.Â  That was my life.Â  "The good ole days" I call them.Â  And once I was on my own I didn't get called names for taking it easy when I had headaches. And I should mention every single time I drank a lick of alcohol, I pay and suffer the most, for days, so I stopped that a long time ago. I can't even smell it anymore without triggering a headache.
Now, this is important to the study you are doing.Â  At 21, my parents took my kids for the weekend and I had my first magic mushroom experience.Â  I loved it.Â  I loved it so much that I left my kids with family once or twice a year and experienced it throughout my entire 20's.Â  During my 20's, the migraines were very few and far between.Â  I thought I had "outgrown" the severe, daily headaches and thought I outgrew being so sensitive to everything.Â  I never considered that the mushrooms had anything to do with it.

This is the first time I have posted here.   I have been visiting the clusterbusters site off an on for the past couple of years - but I have never felt compelled to post anything here until now.  Every time I read a post where someone describes what they are going through I can relate.  Some of the stories here are really sad - and I hope these people take the busters' advice because its all good and has helped me immensely.  I have been reduced to tears reading many of the stories because I know what it is like, and I have had help and I am finally making progress and I there might be some light at the end of this very dark tunnel I have been living in for years,
I have been dealing with CH now for 13 years.  For the first couple of years I was told that I had a a severe case of Sinusitis and that was what was causing my pain.  The doc would give me some Avamys or some other nasal cortosoid, some antibiotics and send me on my way.  This 'worked' for a while as my clusters only lasted for a couple of  weeks and I figured that the sinus infection had passed and I was fine again.  This happened two to three times a year.  No biggie.   The attacks became worse and less tolerable as time went on.   I started to suspect that I did not have sinusitis - even though I felt like I had been kicked in the face and my sinuses were plugged.  At the time I did not have a regular doctor and if I showed up at the hospital or urgent care facility suffering from a CH I was often dismissed as 'drug seeking' and sent home, because the time that any doctor decided to see me the CH had passed and I was feeling 'normal' again.  I did eventually find a doctor who diagnosed me with CH, but he did not know too much about it and treated it like a standard migraine.   Maxalt as an abortive and amytripaline as a preventative - that didn't work, but at least I had a name for what I was dealing with now.  10 years ago the internet was not what it is now.  Cluster Headaches were less known and there were virtually no options for people.  My CH was episodic for 8 years - then they starting getting worse.  The cycles got longer and more frequent, until I got to the point where I am now.   Chronic CH - 4-6 times a day for the past  five years.  I get my 'breaks' from time to time where I will experience a one or two days CH free and I have good days (1 or 2) and I have really bad days (6+ attacks per day).
About three years ago I was fortunate enough to be put in touch with the CHAMP (Calgary Headache Assessment and Management Program) in Calgary.   The team of docs there are nothing short of amazing.  I have had MRI's done, I got a prescription for oxygen (a bit of a struggle to get initially from GP - CHAMP said NO PROBLEM), I got a scrip of Sumitriptan (Immitrex) and they started trying out different preventative meds.   I tried Verapimil.   It didn't really help me, I kept going for my ECG's and all was well until I hit the effective dosage and then BAM the worst tension head

It happen twice in the past before I went episodic on this one.
Once 2003 and once 2009.
In the afermath there where 2 days with a lot of stress. Not much more or different than any other stress.
It started with the same pattern as normal by waking up in the morning taking as usual a cup of coffee. Just after taking the first mug of my prefered drinking, stomache starts to hurt.
The whole abdominal part of the body, think about an oval ballon, pain starts within this ballon, floating and concentrating in the solarus plexus, growing fast to severe pain before exploding at the peak of pain and sending this pain to the outside of the ballon where it floats for short time and then fades away.
Two or three second, pause the same process repeats again and again for 6 to 8 hours, inrcreasing painlevel up to severe.  :'(
I allways ended up in hospital as I couln't stay the pain and pain killers not available or not working/strong enough.
This was in switzerland and medication was fairly quick and apropriate. Whatever they injected, pain went away fast and permanent. First time I stayed over nigth and got different medication during nigth time.
A lot of questions from doctors as they couldn't find any explanation/diagnosis, from bloot test to gastric ulcer they where irritated by the level of pain they couldn't explain. Never seen so far.
I forgot about them until autumn 2011. Same pattern same level of pain start hitting me once or twice a week or even more often.
It was when I discovered my prefered treatment: hot but verry hot shower directly onto my belly always helps to stand pain better while waiting medication to kick in.

Hello all - I'm new to the forum and thought i would share my recent experiences. So as to hopefully help some ppl
1st a little history & a
very large caveat
-- (there are parts of what i went through and meds i took that i would not ever recommend to anyone but i was at my end & it helped for years but i found better now and it is all behind me..(part precautionary tale i guess))
I have had Migraines since 12yrs old & the chronic headache started about 15-16yrs old. At the time i used to just take a lot of advil 9-12 a day because it was all that seemed to settle the pain. And this was when imitrex was injection only and i really hate shots...but...
(side note headaches run through my family so i was prepared a bit for what was too come..... just not that ready....)
Around the age of 20 i came to realize that smoking weed seemed to do the same job as advil so i stopped with the advil and just smoked weed in the AM/PM and it seemed to lower the pain to around 1 for at least 6-8 hours then rinse repeat.
I honestly can not recall any time in the last 15 yrs that my pain level was at 0 (I'm 30 now btw)
It was at about 27 that things took a turn for the worse. I was at work one day and out of nowhere i thought a huge migraine was coming but thought it odd as it had never switched sides before and this was a R sided pain like i had never felt before that dropped me to the ground in the back room. It went on like this for some weeks before i finally made an apt for a neuro as this was all new to me. At this point i had never heard the term Suicide Headache but i knew something was wrong. The 1st Dr. seemed to think it was medicine causing rebound regaurless of how many times i told him that before that day in late spring i had not taken any form of medicine besides antibiotics for a chest infection in almost 5 yrs. So off to the next DR.. 3 later i finally heard the words Suicide Headache and started looking all over the internet for any signs of relief for me... At this point it was unclear whether the CH were episodic or Chronic - 1 year later it was clear that it was not stopping - roughly 5-7 headaches a day when its at its worst down to 1 a day when times are good - This is also on top of Migraine and the usual head pain.
During that 1st year i was trying everything except psychedelics and narcotics. (any further info on what i have tried that did not work please do not hesitate to ask but its tooooo much to list out here) Oxygen provides little relief for the severe headaches and by this point i was just living with it but was really close to being fired and that could not happen so i went back to the DR i liked and begged for narcotics
(
another caveat
-- i have a massive tolerance to meds - i have known this for a long time so any dosage listed for the narcotics are a BAD idea for anyone normal)
With some time and a little persistence i was able to get RX for oxycontin and fentanyl. I had started w/ Norco but was soon to realize i needed a lot

Hi forum, very glad to find this website!
After reading the various stories, I feel like many were like my own situation. So here is my story...very long and not especially extraordinary, but I thought to include all details in case it helps any other newbie someday (e.g. hey that is just like me). So sorry for the length of post.
I started experiencing clusters when I was 18yr in my first yr university, in spring (March-April) several times a day lasting for 4-6weeks. Unbelievable pain (always right side, started with shadows as I now know them to be called, then jaw and neck pain, then spreading to eye socket, felt like a tapered wooden peg, the kind to kill a vampire with,  being hammered in to the eye socket and out through back of skull, very bad), I took OTC pain meds (tylenol overlap with ibuprofen) but did nothing. Saw family doc, thought it was allergies, sent me to dentist but nothing there, sent me to optometrist (since I mentioned eye pain) and got crappy very low prescription. By that time, the cluster episode was over.
Then I did not have another for a few years, then came back again with same pattern and same thing (tried OTC paid meds, saw family doc, blah blah, run-around until cluster over. Then forgot about until next one, again 3yrs later again in the spring, very painful and was done university and working outdoors on the road travelling doing environmental tests out in the field, so could not call in sick, suffered through for the episode of 4-6 weeks. (Note: for the theory of low testosterone and low vit D...I am not sure about, I mean I was outside in the sun almost always during this work for 15hrs per day, and I was in my early 20s working out in gym like crazy, really good libido, so think my vit D and testosterone was fine, but was getting crappy sleep so totally could buy the upset biological clock connection)
Same pattern with episodes every 2-3yrs typically in spring for 4-6weeks with max frequency/day in middle of cluster, just suffered through and probably almost OD'ing on OTC pain meds which did almost nothing.
Then in my mid 30's when I was on parental leave for my first child and was the only care provider at home, when a cluster epodes started. I knew I could not watch this baby with multiple attacks daily, so went to ER hoping they could figure out. This is in Ottawa, Canada, I went to a larger hospital which is part of University of Ottawa med program, so very good competent people up to date on everything. I had done some homework by now and had suspected my headaches were CH. I had taken very good notes over the years and tracked the dates and duration of he cluster episodes the location and type of pain, etc. So told ER doc this and they thought for sure CH, put me on O2 in the ER and tried lidocaine into nasal passage? Thursday December 8, 2011

2004 - 2006
12-14 yrs old
Somewhere during this time the headaches start every day at 11am, 2pm, and 5pm.
Try everything from Tylenol to ibuprofen find out ONLY Excedrin Migraine works.
Start taking it at the onset of a headache. At 5pm - if the Excedrin worked i was headache free the remainder of the night. If it failed to work i kept the headache until 11am the next morning.
2007
15 years old
Start documenting my headaches for doctor. Tests imitrex, skelaxin, and some other scripts. Says it is hormone related so starts me on Depo Pro-Vera birth control shot. 1 shot every 3 months. Stops my periods completely. At some point during this time I start waking up with a headache. Continue taking Excedrin Migraine. Didnt not document figured i was stuck either way so i dont know the times of having them besides every morning and at other points later in the day. Just took medicine when i had one and that was it.
Referred to a neurologist.
I undergo an EEG, multiple blood tests, MRI. All come back with nothing. He diagnoses me with Chronic Cluster Headaches. Puts me on some random prescription. This medicine makes it 10 times worse, makes me feel drugged and makes my emotions go crazy.After 2 weeks i stop taking and stick with my excedrin. I tell the neuro i will no longer take any prescriptions because im tired of these pills messing with my and not fixing my headaches. He prescribes the same thing so i cease going to him.
2008
16 years old
only taking Excedrin Migraine every morning and every time a headache comes around. I realize if the Excedrin doesnt work any of the times i take i am stuck with the headache the remainder of the day no matter what i do. Some are worse than others. Most are bearable.. i guess. Didnt have a choice. Some though i couldnt even walk around without wanting to cut my head off.
2009
17 years old
Start seeing a Chiropractor. first 3 times a week, then twice a week, then once, then twice a month, then as needed. Brought my headaches from the 3+ times a day down to ONCE every single morning upon wake up. I take the excedrin migraine every single morning. Same as 2008 if it works its gone, if not im stuck. There are random days (well probably not random) that they come later in the day from one time to 4 times. for the most part it was every single morning. I HIGHLY RECOMMEND A CHIROPRACTOR!! TOOK ME FROM 3+ DOWN TO ONCE DAILY! (most days)
2010
18 years old.
I come to the realization that it wasnt until i started the Depo Prov-era that i started waking up with a headache every morning. I cease getting the shot. Hello all.  I wanted to share a little of my story with clusters.  I should have probably posted this a little while back, but its hard to sometimes relive all of it. I apologize for how long this is.  I should work on a cliff notes version.
Everything started in January of 2014.  I started waking up during sleep every hour or two with the most intense pain i had ever felt in and around my right eye.  The pain shot through my head and went down the right side of my neck and last about 30 minutes to an hour.  At first I figured I must be sleeping wrong.  First I tried new pillows, but nothing changed.  Then I thought maybe a chiropractor could help, but was of course wrong.  The pain kept hitting me every night, and consistently every 2 hours after I went to sleep.  Finally went to a doctor after a month.  Up to this point the headaches only came on at night.  During the day everything was fine except me being a little exhausted.  The doctor didn't really have any ideas and wanted me to go to physical therapy.  I knew he was wrong and never did go.  Started looking for a new doctor after that.
The new doctor was much more helpful, but still didn't have any ideas.  Then one night we had a breakthrough that helped pinpoint the problem.  This is something that is hard to explain.  One night shortly after my first visit with the new doctor I was in the kitchen trying to ride one of the headaches out.  There was a voice in my head.  It just said take the medicine in the junk drawer.  My wife used to suffer from migraines, but they stopped about 6 months before my clusters.  Unknown to me one of her old maxalt tabs was in that drawer.  I took it and the headache melted away within 5 minutes.  It never came back that night.
With that new information and a little googling my wife and I came across cluster headaches.  All the symptoms lined up exactly with what I had been dealing with.  On the next visit I explained all this to the new doc.  She said she had also done some research and wanted to talk to me about cluster headaches.  This was when I was given my first prescription for verapamil.
The verapamil helped, but about this time my clusters also changed.  Instead of just getting them at night I started getting them during the day.  I also started experiencing my first shadows about this time.  Headaches were always about the same time.  The cycles would drift some.  Looser schedule and less intense, but slowly becoming fixed at the same times each day and much more intense.  Then the cycle would fade and start over.  During this time I was getting the headaches pretty much every day.  There was the occasional random day where I was pain free, but it was rare.
Things continued like this until October of that first year.  I dealt with the pain and headaches as much as possible and was using Maxalt as an abortive.  I also had access to some shots that were left from my wife's issues with migraines.  In the middle of October things shot up to 11.  I had

Hello everyone, this is my first post as I am new to the community. I’ll first share my story trying to be brief (I won’t/can't).
I’ve separated my post in a first presentation part, and in a second part where i talk about my recent busting experience. English is not my native language but don't hesitate to correct me if I am gramatically wrong.
My name is Serge, 32 years old, from Geneva in Switzerland. I am born from a Romanian father and a Guatemalan mother. I’ve been in couple for more than 10 years and married for almost 5. I have two boys 5 and 6 years old. I’ve had headaches from as long as I could remember, but i couldn’t identify patterns. They were just so bad and no doctor was able to understand it.
My cluster journey really started in september 2018, we went to spain in vacation and everyday I got terrible headaches. When we got back home, they were becoming worse, showing up at night, at work, it started having a really bad impact on my life. After two years of suffering almost everyday, i wasn’t strong enough to keep my job.
I was a gate and check-in supervisor at the local airport, I had a good carreer there on the rise, but it came to a stop. In this job, you need to be fully available, you work early and late, you can't be absent even for a few minutes if you're in pain, so it wasnt compatible with my shit condition.
I’ve kept trying to go back to work after some resting periods, and it never worked, the intense stress, hard schedules, made my pain worsening when back at work.
When you are on sick leave for more than 3 months, the swiss law requires people to apply for disability. therefore, during a very long process, I’ve been sent to make some medical and psychologicyl exams to definite if I can still work or not.
It was around the same time that a proper cluster headache diagnosis had been made. Before this i was suggested (and convinced) that I had arnold neuralgia, I’ve already had tried all the numerous migraine medications you know.I had already tried nerve decompression in the back of the head too, with no results of course.
In late 2020 the examinations i underwent concluded that i had borderline personality disorders, and confirmed the cluster headache diagnosis.
At this moment when I had this annoucement I was trying to work again at the airport, but it didn’t went well, my wife and I lost our 5 weeks old daughter and that sent me into an even deeper depression, leading to the end of my contract.
The state soon declared me 100% invalid. In Switzerland when this happens, your salary is paid by retirment office and by occupational pension. Because i have children, I get monthly roughly the same amount of money that when I used to work my 40 weekly hours.
It took time to reconstruct, but at the same time I met a better neurologist (the one that properly diagnosed me) that prescibed me isoptin, oxygen and cymbalta, and as triptans, zomig 5mg nasal spray. At first and until recently it worked pretty well, almost all pain a

Greetings!
Hello folks. I have been lingering around this board and the clusterheadaches.com board since 2001. It was at that time, when I was 16 years old, I was diagnosed with clusterheadache. It is hard to imagine that soon I will have had these things for half of my lifetime, and have still not found the best treatment for me. I guess it is just an evolution. I hope to use this thread to detail my work thus far to combat my current cycle, as well as the work I still have ahead of me. Feel free to comment, questions, follow along, whatever you'd like.
My Background Previous to Current Cycle: (Feel free to skip over)
I had what I thought were migraines only ever once in a while (one every few years) as a young child. Didn't think a whole lot about it. Then I had them three days in a row in 2001 at the exact same time each day and thought "Holy shit". I thought I was going to die. I was fortunate enough to receive a CH diagnosis right then from my family practitioner. He prescribed a prednisone taper, Inderal, and Zomig. The Prednisone and the inderal killed the cycle. I had a few more cycles over the next year and this medication mix worked just fine. Then things got more interesting:

Â  Through my own research I found out about busting. I used MM and did not get another attack until January 2004.
- Winter 2004: I used MM and killed the cycle in its tracks.
- Fall of 2004: I entered a cycle that was not responding to MM. I can't remember exactly how the cycle ended. I do remember this being an extremely agonizing period for me. I also started using oxygen at this time, however, I was not using it properly and therefor didn't receive much relief with it.
- Fall of 2005: I had high hopes about Kudzu at this time. For some reason thought this would be my silver bullet. When it was not working, I can't remember what else I was turning to as a preventative. I do remember this cycle being extremely agonizing. I remember doing crazy stuff like running through the freezing cold in a tank top and slamming energy drinks.
- Winter 2006: I don't remember the particulars about how I tried treating this cycle either. I'm assuming I was trying to bust with LSA seeds or shrooms. I remember what I was doing with my life, but not the CH treatment.
- Summer 2008: This was my first time in the professional, working world trying to combat CH. I believe I tried medical doctor approach again. I know I did a prednisone taper as well as inderal. I recall a few attacks shining through even when on the prednisone. I was blow away. I was trying RC seeds at this time, I believe.

I am 47 years old and I have been suffering almost daily, and typically 2 or 3 times a day, from headaches on the right side of my head for six and a half months now.  First I had my teeth checked, then got a new prescription in my glasses.  Neither helped... Most often they initiate at a point just above the temple but slightly closer to my eye and about 1/2 inch beneath my skull.  It is a sharp point source that radiates outward and it is almost always in the exact same location.  I was initially treating the headaches with Ibuprofen and was tricked into believing that it helped, because the headaches would only last 30 or 40 minutes and I was attributing the relief to the Ibuprofen.  I suspected that the Ibuprofen had stopped working so I moved on to Naproxen then started stacking Extra Strength Tylenol.  After about 2 months of constant suffering I ended up resorting to the ER, where I had CT scans both with and without contrast.  Both were negative.  I then found a new doctor and was treated with oral Prednisone 40mg which had no effect on the headache, but turned off my ability to control myself, culminating in smashing my guitar.  So 6 days into the Prednisone I messaged my doctor and he told me to stop the Prednisone.  Subsequently, we tried Sumatriptan Nasal Spray 20mg.  I did not get relief so I gave up on it after 2 attempts.  Later, I returned to the Sumatriptan, using 10 doses in 8 days only to realize that I was being tricked again in the same way as I described with the Ibuprofen.  I was then scheduled for an MRI which was also negative.
I had heard about psilocybin mushrooms as a treatment for cluster headaches and have tried several times.  The mushrooms always completely turn the headaches off for the duration, then significantly reduce the intensity, duration and frequency of the attacks for a few days.  Today my doctor finally prescribed oxygen as an abortive and Topamax as a preventative.  I am afraid to start taking the Topamax, as I was only just able to stop taking antidepressants (primarily Zoloft) 20 months ago, after having taken them for 20 years for severe depression.  I know that the Topamax will interfere with psilocybin as well, so I don't think I'm going to take it.  I have high hopes for oxygen therapy but it was a real hassle trying to explain how to write the prescription.  Then, the medical supply company that he gave the order to wanted to fix me up with an oxygen concentrator.  Fortunately, I refused and was able to go back into the office and request a different provider that could supply a type M tank, regulator and mask.  I am waiting on the insurance to approve the treatment....
I'm convinced that these are cluster headaches, but I am worried that my headaches are different from cluster, and seeking feedback regarding symptoms.  So about 80% of my attacks last about 30 to 40 minutes and I would describe my level of pain at around 6.  I think this number might be diminished by a high tolerance to pain, bu
