text:
Hi fellow sufferers,
Two months ago I was diagnosed with cluster headaches. I’m 29 years old. It started in mid-November. At first the attacks were “moderate” and always came at 10 a.m. After about a week, the intensity increased. The attacks became more severe and now came at 10 a.m. and 3 p.m., usually lasting 1–2 hours.
I felt it in my temple, my jaw, above my eye, below my eye, in my nose, at the back of my head, and on my scalp. It felt like someone was hammering a huge nail from top to bottom straight through my face. I could even feel it in my palate. I get goosebumps just thinking about it. I thought I was going to die or had a brain tumor or something similar. At first there were only the attacks, and afterwards I was pain-free again.
After 5–6 severe attacks, a one-sided constant pain above my eye set in, which lasted 24/7, and the attacks seemed to arise out of that. The pain first brought me to my family doctor, who couldn’t help and sent me to the hospital after three days with no improvement. At that point I was taking ibuprofen like candy with no effect. I still kept taking it because I thought: at some point it has to work.
At the hospital I described my symptoms to the neurologist: a blocked nose, a watery eye, and head and facial pain that I didn’t think a human being could have and survive. She immediately recognized it: cluster headaches. I was then treated with triptan injections. The attacks were stopped, but the constant headache remained.
I had never heard of this disease before and started reading about it while still in the hospital. I used to be a smoker and quit the same day. Later I was treated at a pain clinic that had experience with cluster headaches. I kept a pain diary and read more and more about it. I started supplementing D3K2. And I found out that bright light triggers the attacks. The intensity of the attacks became somewhat lower—until they finally disappeared after about two weeks. However, the constant headache remained and I began to develop very intense light sensitivity. From then on, a baseball cap and sunglasses were my best friends.
I remembered that I had something similar about three years ago, though not with this intensity of attacks. Back then they thought I had sinusitis and I thought my wisdom teeth were simply causing problems. It was already intense enough at the time that they did a CT scan and found nothing. But it wasn’t comparable to the attacks now, and back then I didn’t even realize they were “attacks.” But I do remember the same constant headache above the eye that lasted for weeks and then simply disappeared from one day to the next. That’s when it became clear to me that it had already been cluster back then, knocking for the first time.
To get rid of the constant headache and end the episode for good, the doctor at the pain clinic prescribed prednisolone. He said normally they would also add verapamil, but since the attacks were largely over, it wasn’t necessary (thank God). In t

Most contributors to this forum will be far more knowledgeable than I regarding both cluster headaches and those substances which have not as yet become mainstream weapons for the medical community in the fight against this uniquely painful affliction.
I told my 25-year-old son Michael's story on Clusterbusters sister site, Clusterheadaches. I repeat it here in case it could be of benefit to any sufferers out there looking for others with similar symptoms and histories who might not luck onto, as I did, the sister site. Bonkers is the screen name used on that site as well.
Michael got hit out of the blue one day while taking a shower. It knocked him to the ground, hurt like hell and scared the shit out of him. He was 12 years old. It wasn't the last one. We thought they were migraines since both my mother and I had those from our teens until we outgrew them at around 30. Our family doctor tried a variety of headache medicines on Michael over the next couple of years without success. He referred us to a Neurologist who, after conducting the usual tests, diagnosed the headaches as Clusters. He tried most, if not all, of the standard cluster remedies. Imitrex seemed to work best, but it too lost effectiveness over time. It got to the point where 2 shots did nothing. Michael got the most relief, and continues to get the most relief, from narcotics. Most of the time they deaden the pain enough that he doesn't black out. He's currently taking Methadone. Unlike the other narcotics, it hasn't required continually increasing dosages to be effective. The neuro continued experimenting with the various cluster remedies, including narcotics, until Michael was about 20 years old.
Then, he got into a particularly nasty car accident. He was life-flighted to the hospital with head injuries. Nothing too serious, but his headaches increased exponentially in both frequency and severity. He was uncontrollable during the spikes. Luckily, the pain usually increased to the point where he would black out. By now he was in the emergency room every couple of days where they've got some bigger guns. Nothing helped. They had to put him in a straight-jacket and eventually, with consent from the neuro, a drug-induced coma. They kept him there for a week. When Michael emerged from the coma he seemed better for awhile. But then they returned. Frustrated, the neuro decided Michael should take all the medicines at once! I don't think we even said goodbye. Degenerative disc disease and 5 fused vertebrae in my neck had me seeing a neck and back pain specialist who I trusted and considered a very good doctor. I brought my son to see him. He's been caring for Michael for the last 4 or 5 years
Michael's case is rare, I believe, in its severity. Not to trivialize the extreme suffering of others, but Michael's spikes often intensify until they result in his blacking out, sometimes as often as 30 times/day. He can't work. He can't go to school. He has no social life. He lays in bed, in a

(Sorry this is a bit long, but I wanted to be thorough about this problem I've had for years.)
I've made my first attempt at busting my headaches, but so far am not completely successful.
My history:
I've suffered from debilitating headaches for years. It started mainly after I graduated college and moved to a high altitude city that is very dry and was under a lot of stress to get going professionally. I started having what I believed were sinus headaches. Over the counter remedies helped at first, things like Sinutuab, or Excedrin with Sudafed. However, over time those stopped working. My doctor thought I was having migraines and therefore prescribed me triptans such as Axert and Maxalt. Those worked, but left me loopy, but with too much use, they started rebounding.
So we tried preventing headaches with the antidepressant, Amitriptyline. That worked, but it left me very drowsy all day long and it made it hard to get up in the morning. I kept oversleeping and was often late to work. I nearly got fired. So I quit that and starting using Migrin during the day which sometimes stopped my headaches and sometimes didn't. After a while they quit making it and I switched to Immitrex, which worked but I got caught in a cycle of rebound headaches.
So I went to a pain specialist clinic and they tried a number of headache preventatives. Toprimate worked, but made me so forgetful I could barely remember anything. One day I went to endorse a check, but I couldn't remember how to sign my name. My roommate claimed I went into his room and threatened to kill him the previous night, but I had no memory of that. So me doctor took me off that and put me on Depakote. That worked, but it made me sleep 12 hours a night and then need at least a 1 hour nap during the day. Even when I was awake, I was always drowsy and forgetful. It was practically impossible to even work. So I went off Depakote and had horrible withdrawals, day after day of intense pain. Now I'm off it and can concentrate and sleep normal hours, but the headaches are back.
I've found I can sometimes relieve the pain with self hypnosis, but that's limited. If you're working somewhere, you can't take an hour off to go into a deep trance. If I'm at home, deep hypnosis will sometimes get rid of my headaches for the rest of the day, but sometimes it only relieves it while I'm in the trance. I do daily hypnosis to try a cumulative effect and prevent the headaches. But I still have problems. The headaches still come and run my life.

I did self hypnosis twice to make sure I was in a very calm and happy state of mind. I had bought 8 grams from a trusted source and had turned it all into capsules. First, I am sorry for the long post but I felt compelled to share my results with the group.  Also forgive my rambling style.
Many, many thanks to everyone on this site for the vast amount of knowledge and information sharing that go on constantly.  I have suffered from episodic Clusters for over 30 years.  I had not had a cycle for the last 7 years until September of this year.  I really thought they were gone forever.  So, off to my GP to load up on Imitrex and a new O2 script.  I did order the new Cluster O2 mask and picked up a size M tank.  Reading all of the busting stories perked my interest in trying to find a way to minimize or stop the cycle completely.  I ordered RC seeds from TrancePlants and I fully intended to try MoxieGirlÂ’s preparation procedure.  I also went through the terrible process of detoxing to get off the Imitrex as I wanted to insure the best chance of this procedure working.  I could not have survived without the oxygen and the 5 hour energy shots. In the meantime I ran across the D3 Regime by Batch and started on it.  It really seemed to help as after only 5 days, hits went down from 3-4 per day and another 2-3 at night to only one or two a day only one each night.  The intensity also seemed a bit less.  I should add that I also began taking 10mg of Melatonin each night at bedtime.
The seeds arrived and I prepared 35 of them (Thanks MoxiGirl) and I used them Â½ hour before bedtime on a Thursday night. I didnÂ’t put lemon in the mixture; I just drank it and chased it with a small glass of OJ.  Within 30 minutes, I could feel a very small buzz (no nausea) and I went to bed.  I was very apprehensive about tripping which is why I only used 35.  I slept all night without being awakened by the beast which had not happened in a while.  Even the next morning hit which usually occurred at 8-9 AM did not arrive.  However at 1:30 PM I was hit with a kip 6-7.  No more hits during the evening and I slept all night again.  During the next 3-4 days I had the exact symptoms everyone describes in Â“SlapbacksÂ”.  Pains were occurring at odd times with varying duration and frequency.  Something definitely was having an effect.  For the most part I did sleep all night each evening with only a couple of mild bearable hits.

Hello everyone,
First time posting. I am so glad I found this site and have gotten to read so many stories similar to mine. ItÂ’s good to know that I am not alone and there is a group of well informed/supportive people out there. I also love the fact that people have found alternative methods for curing/relieving the pain. Anyhow, IÂ’m not sure if I am posting this in the right place but I feel the need to share it.
This year started off as one of the worst years of my life. Sure, I had been getting these episodes for over 10 years, but it had been almost two years without any and now the attacks were back and more frequent and intense then ever (one to two attacks every day for nearly 5 months).
I had seen my doctor about the attacks in the past but was always told they were just migraines or tension headaches. She told me to take some OTC meds and was once given a migraine prescription med, which did nothing. Due to the meds really having no effect, and because I was always reluctant to take pharmaceutical drugs, most of the time I would just Â“tough it outÂ”. After trying the tough it method during the episode early this year, I had finally reached my breaking point. The CH came for a 5th day in a row while I was at work. I told my boss that I couldnÂ’t work like this any longer and that I would be taking time off to pursue treatment of this condition. I walked out of work that day and immediately called my doctor.
Deep down I knew what the condition was but I was too afraid to admit it. I had done research a few years back and my symptoms aligned with the condition known as Cluster Headaches. I was in denial at the time and never told anyone what I thought it was. From what I read, there was no magic pill to cure it, so I did not want to admit to myself that I had an incurable condition. I simply tried to ignore it.
However, on that day I went to my doctorÂ’s office and decided it was time to confront the beast. I told my doctor they were not migraines but were Cluster Headaches. She said she didnÂ’t know much about it and then referred me to a neurologist. The neurologist said it sounded possible and after he left the room for 10 minutes he came back and confirmed it was CH. Finally, it was like a weight lifted off my shoulders. It was a day a dreaded but finally putting a name to the horrible condition gave me some sore of relief.

Overall, the side effects from all the drugs had me feeling like a zombie and I was still getting the attacks. After a month without any relief I went back to the Doc and told him about the side effects, and he simply gave me different abortive medication and upped the verapamil dosage. I have not posted on this site before, but have been skulking around it for years and I figure I may as well add our story because without so many of you (who don't even know it!) things would be so much different!
My husband is an episodic cluster sufferer.  Started in 1995 and came on hard and fast.  "Suicide headache" is about the only appropriate term for what he would suffer.  (And I loved the GP whom I tried to explain this to who suggested my husband might need to be checked into a facility if he was throwing around terms like suicide.)  My husband would get headaches every 6 or 12 or 18 months and the doctors didn't diagnose it for years.  It was a TERRIBLE time for him.
It took years before we got the cluster diagnosis and finally found Prednisone which ... kind of ... helped.  The steroid made him crazy.  Literally.  Aggressive, impatient, angry and mad which are polar opposites of his character.  Also, many times, the clusters would 'break through' when he weaned down to 10mg (after starting at 70 or 80!) and he would have to start the prednisone all over again.
10 years ago he found oxygen and that was a godsend.  Together with Imitrex and handfuls of whatever other pills were in the house, I could keep him vaguely able to handle the intense amount of pain that gripped him.  It was especially fun when our son was born and he was hit with the worst cluster ever (Spring 2003) and couldn't tolerate any noise at all.
We continued at this pace until 2008 when he was hit in March.  Prednisone allowed breakthroughs FOUR times and he was still having headaches in August.  The doctors (GP and Neuro) kept prescribing ever increasing doses of Prednisone - together with Topamax (migraines) and Verapamil (tension headaches) which had never done him any good.  One night when I was about at the end of my tether, I found this website - and it changed everything.
We did the cleansing, hopped on the Vitamin M trail, and never looked back!  Every three months kept them at bay the first year.  Every four months and nothing the second year!  Really, it was like a miracle!!  It has been hard for me not to stand on a soap box and shout this out to everyone I meet - but instead - we remain quietly at home, trying to keep it on the down low.  He never saw his neurologist after that because he didn't want to have this kind of thing in his medical file.
Enter Thanksgiving this year.  We had grown complacent and moved to a six month maintenance schedule for October.  We hit mid month and he realized he had a tiny bit of Vitamin M left.  He took it - but it had no effect.  I literally WATCHED the headaches come over the next few days.  His right eye will begin to droop just a bit and then grow more and more flat.  We only have one place to procure our vitamins and there was a major miscommunication.

Hi,
My name is Ton, I live in Amsterdam. I have been having cluster headaches for about 15+ years I guess, but I only started getting proper 'cluster periods' about 8 years ago. ItÂ  was always relatively mild, till about 4 years ago, when I started seeing doctor about it. Had some tests etc and finally was sent to a neurologist who diagnosed CH. The final diagnosis was at the beginning of a cluster 2 years ago.
My neurologist advised verapamil, and sumatriptan for acute treatment. Near the end of the cluster I was taking 960 mg of verapamil a day (which I have been told is a ridiculous amount) and almos every night the maximum allowed 2 injections of sumatriptan. My cluster subsided then, but it had been going on for months so there's a good chance it had nothing to do with the verapamil. In fact, this medically treated cluster was the WORST THING EVER. Lasted a lot longer than previous clusters, my headaches were way worse, and for the first time I started having attacks during the day, and bad ones. Oxygen helped a bit, it shortened the attacks but rarely really aborted the bad ones. This was not a route I wanted to go down again.
So when I noticed a nasty headache a month ago I immediately went online and ordered a (cubensis) mushroom grow kit (fyi: it is not legal to sell magic mushrooms in Holland any more but growkits are legal). By the time my mushrooms were ready I was in full cluster, and I took my first dose 2 weeks ago. I had never actually tried magic mushrooms before, so I decided what the heck, if you're gonna do it, at least have some fun :-) So together with a friend who had some experience I made tea (the recipe with lemon juice/vitamine C/instant coffee) and we had a fun evening. I took about 2.5 grams of dried mushroom and had what I guess was a mild trip (I'm a big guy, 115 kilo. my 75 kilo friend who took the same dose experienced a lot more), lots of bad humor etc. And that night I slept quite well, only had a mild headache in the morning. The next 4 nights I had headaches, but a lot milder than the week before, then I had two pretty bad nights. As I wanted to time my intake with the weekend I waited till friday again before taking my next dose, 2 grams as I want to find the point where it is effective without having a full on trip. Still quite dizzy, and had some headaches that night, and the following days, worse than after I took the first dose. This wednesday (decided to do the 5 day thing) took 1.5 grams (did not add the instant coffee to the tea mixture this time) and to my surprise I felt more out of it that the last time with 2 grams. Could be a difference in theÂ  mushrooms, the first batch I dried on a heater, which I read later may have hurt the potency. Had a bad night, and thursday was quite shadowy. But thursday my oxygen arrived, so last night whenever I woke up with the beginning of a headache I hit the oxygen and was fine in a couple of minutes, did not even get close to a 'real' headache.
